title: The Message in the Machine
subtitle: A docent story from the Vintage Computer Museum at InfoAge
per_page_duration_rule: HARD constraint: every storyboard page is on screen between 10.0s and 15.0s. No page under 10s, no page over 15s. Adjacent narration beats are merged onto one image to reach >=10s and split before 15s; where a clean sentence break conflicts with the window, the window wins. Achieved range: min 10.0s, max 14.5s, avg ~12.1s.

page_distribution._note: By the segment a page opens in; four pages are transition pages whose narration crosses a script-segment boundary (P4, P9, P12, P23, P32).

narration_source.note: This video REUSES the finished VO track from the podcast-studio project. No new narration is rendered for the video.

timing_notes.v17_retime: Script v17 ADDED a sixth spoken line to Segment 5 ('And somebody actually called that number. A bootlegger, mid-copy, picked up the phone and dialed Tokyo for help.') and seg-05-ikegami was re-rendered (surgical; segs 1-4/6/7 reused). Use the durations.json _post_atempo keys directly (real as-heard durations on episode-vo.mp3, no 1.06x rescaling). Seg 5 post-atempo = 62.01s (was 60.32s). New total _total = 424.77s (7:04.8), up from 423.08s.
timing_notes.page_timing_method: Each page duration is computed from its narration character count at each segment's real (post-atempo) speaking rate, then beats are merged/split so every page lands in [10.0, 15.0]s. Page in/out times are contiguous (no gaps/overlaps) and the final page ends at ~07:04.8. Pages P1-P22 are frozen from the prior cut (Segments 1-4, unchanged audio); P23-P35 were re-paged for the new 6-line Segment 5 and the new total.

visual_style.audience: potential museum visitors; retro-gaming and computer-history curious
visual_style.tone: warm, personal, nostalgic, documentary; NOT flashy/shorts-style; no baked-in clickbait text
visual_style.era_1981_basement: warm, grainy, tungsten light, cigarette haze, period-accurate (TRS-80, CRT glow, PROM programmer, jumper wires, Donkey Kong cabinet)
visual_style.era_present_museum: InfoAge old radar-building interior, rows of vintage computers, docent POV
visual_style.climax: Seg 4 hex-dump reveal designed as actual hex-dump screens with right-hand ASCII column, building to the message appearing; on-screen text used for the reveal (P21-P22)
visual_style.documented_message_seg5: Show the verified v17 ROM text on screen (P27), opening with CONGRATULATION: 'CONGRATULATION ! IF YOU ANALYSE DIFFICULT THIS PROGRAM, WE WOULD TEACH YOU. ***** TEL.TOKYO-JAPAN 044(244)2151 EXTENTION 304 SYSTEM DESIGN IKEGAMI CO. LIM.'
visual_style.new_v17_beat: Seg 5 line 6 ('somebody actually called that number') shares the closing Seg 5 page (P28), carried by a new bootlegger-calls-Tokyo image.
visual_style.facts: Donkey Kong (Nintendo, 1981); Ikegami Tsushinki as uncredited shadow developer; Ikegami won the copyright suit. No invented claims.

image_assets.convention: assets/images/generated/pNN-slug.png and assets/images/real/REAL-slug.png; pNN matches the page number unless an already-generated file was preserved across a renumber.

existing_preserved.p31-courtroom-rom-evidence.png: now page 30 (filename keeps old p31 number)
existing_preserved.p36-infoage-exterior-closing.png: now page 35 (filename keeps old p36 number)

new_for_v17.p28b-bootlegger-calls-tokyo.png: page 28 (NEW — to be generated; bootlegger mid-copy dialing Tokyo)

real_photo_candidates: Sand Hill Vending / family arcade photo — home basement arcade (P5); secondary: P2 (family owned arcade games)
a real Donkey Kong cabinet, ideally one at InfoAge (P15)
a period family photo of Nick at ~16 (P23)
the actual TRS-80 in the InfoAge collection (P33)
